title: Giant nonlinear conductivity in 2D electron gas from substrate-induced dipolar scattering
authors: Dmitry V. Chichinadze, Alexander Seidel, Zohar Nussinov
abstract: Despite a surge of interest in the nonlinear transport in 2D materials, a fundamental puzzle remains: existing theoretical frameworks are unable to quantitatively account for the giant nonlinear conductivities ($\gtrsim 1 \frac{μ\text{m}}{Ω\text{V}}$) recently reported in 2D van der Waals heterostructures. Here, we introduce a mechanism based on electron scattering from a substrate-induced periodic dipole array. We show that the strict kinematic constraints, inherent to 2D scattering, lead to a singular enhancement of the nonlinear response, fundamentally dictating a natural scale of $1 \frac{μ\text{m}}{Ω\text{V}}$.
summary: This paper proposes that the unexpectedly large nonlinear conductivity in 2D materials arises from electrons scattering off periodic electric dipoles in the substrate. The mechanism relies on a mathematical singularity in the scattering gradients, which naturally predicts the observed scale of 1 micro-ohm/volt.
main_problem: Existing theoretical frameworks cannot quantitatively explain the massive second-order nonlinear conductivities observed in 2D van der Waals heterostructures.
main_result: The authors identify a mechanism where electron scattering from a substrate-induced periodic dipole array leads to a singular enhancement of the nonlinear response due to logarithmic divergences in scattering probability gradients.
method: The study uses a Boltzmann equation approach, T-matrix calculations to leading order in the dipole moment, and symmetry analysis to determine the conductivity tensor structure.
model_or_system: A 2D isotropic Fermi gas in the xy-plane subject to both conventional short-range impurity scattering and a static periodic array of electric dipoles originating from a substrate.
key_observables: Linear conductivity (sigma) and nonlinear conductivity tensor components (specifically longitudinal response B and transverse Hall response C).
important_parameters: Dipole moment (d), impurity concentration (n_imp), Fermi momentum (kF), and the screening parameter (xi).
main_assumptions: The 2D electron gas is assumed to be isotropic with a circular Fermi surface, and the dipole array is assumed to be periodic with uniform dipole moments and orientations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of electrons in a 2D gas scattering from substrate dipoles. Figure 2 displays linear conductivity scaling with d^2, nonlinear tensor components as a function of d, and the logarithmic enhancement of nonlinear conductivity with the screening parameter.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the puzzle of giant nonlinearity, defines the physical model of dipole scattering, performs symmetry analysis, derives the nonlinear conductivity using the Boltzmann equation and T-matrix, and concludes with the identification of the singular enhancement mechanism.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic of the physical system. Electrons in a 2D gas (blue) scatter from an array of electric dipoles (purple) originating from the substrate (gray).

Figure 2. (Left) Linear conductivity as a function of dipole moment. (Middle) Components of the nonlinear conductivity tensor as a function of dipole moment, consistent with the symmetry analysis. (Right) Nonzero components of the nonlinear conductivity tensor as a function of ξ (log-linear plot). Solid lines indicate the analytical solution, while scatter points show numerical solution obtained using method of iterations with Direct Inversion in the Iterative Subspace (DIIS) to enforce convergence. The minor discrepancy in ˜σ is due to the logarithmic accuracy of the analytical solution. We used m = 0.5me, τ = 10−11s, µ = 10−2eV, with dy = 0 for all panels; ξ = 10−8 in the left and middle...
